mnemonics.title: B-Tree Properties
mnemonics.content: B-Tree: 'Balanced Trees Keep Disk Accesses Minimized' - B-Trees are Balanced, maintain minimum keys (t-1), Keep all leaves at same level, minimize Disk I/O through high branching factor.

mnemonics.title: Node Split Rule
mnemonics.content: 'Full Split Middle Up' - When a node is full (2t-1 keys), split it and push the middle key up to the parent.

mnemonics.title: Keys and Children
mnemonics.content: 'Keys + 1 = Children' - A node with k keys always has exactly k+1 children. Remember: Keys tell you the gaps, children fill those gaps.

acronyms.expansion: Balanced Tree (or Bayer Tree named after Rudolf Bayer)
acronyms.usage: Used for database indexing and file systems due to minimized disk I/O operations.
Defines the minimum and maximum number of keys: minimum t-1, maximum 2t-1 per node.
Maximum number of children a node can have. Also called 'order' or 'Branching factor'.关系: t = ⌈m/2⌉, m = 2t-1.
Named because nodes can have 2, 3, or 4 children corresponding to 1, 2, or 3 keys.

visualTips.visualization: Draw a box divided into compartments for keys with arrows between them pointing to child nodes. For 3 keys: |Key1|Key2|Key3| → 4 children: C0 < Key1 < C1 < Key2 < C2 < Key3 < C3.
Picture a full glass (4-node) overflowing - the middle element 'spills' up to the parent, leaving two half-full glasses (2-nodes) below.
Imagine all leaf nodes resting on a flat floor - the 'ground'. This visual reminds you all leaves are at the same level (depth).
A B-Tree is like a wide, short building (many floors but each floor has many rooms). A binary tree is tall and narrow. Fewer floors = fewer elevator trips (disk accesses).

analogies.analogy: B-Trees are like a multi-story parking garage with wide floors (high branching). Finding a car (search) requires going to fewer floors (disk reads) than a narrow spiral ramp (binary tree).
Think of B-Tree nodes as filing cabinets. A minimum degree t means: a cabinet must be at least half-full (t-1 files), can hold up to 2t-1 files, and has t-1 to 2t-1 drawers.
When a 4-node splits in a 2-4 tree, it's like splitting a 4-person car into two 2-person cars - the middle person rides with the parent (middle key moves up).
All leaves at the same level is like all rooms in a building being on the ground floor in terms of 'distance from entrance' - you always know how many stairs to climb.
